hero.title: Know when your codebase drifts before it breaks
hero.subtitle: Cross-correlate signals from git, CI, dependencies, and deployments. Local-first, zero-config, evidence-based.

features.title: Why Evolution Engine
features.subtitle: One command gives you visibility that no single tool can provide.
features.local_first_desc: Your code never leaves your machine. All analysis runs locally. Only opt-in telemetry (anonymous usage stats) ever phones home.
features.zero_config_desc: Auto-detects your tools from configs, lockfiles, and imports. No YAML, no dashboards, no setup. Just evo analyze .
features.evidence_based_desc: Statistical deviation from your repo's own baseline. Modified z-scores, not arbitrary thresholds. Calibrated across 43 open-source repos.
features.cross_signal_desc: Correlates git + CI + deps + deploys. "CI fails after big dependency updates" is a pattern no single tool sees.
features.extensible_desc: Open adapter ecosystem. EE discovers available adapters for your tools, notifies you about updates, and auto-pulls community patterns.

how_it_works.title: How it works
how_it_works.subtitle: Three steps. No configuration needed.
how_it_works.step1_desc: One pip install. Works with Python 3.10+.
how_it_works.step2_desc: Run in any git repo. EE auto-detects your tools and computes signals.
how_it_works.step3_desc: Read the advisory, investigate with AI, or set up the GitHub Action for continuous monitoring.

signals.title: Signal Families
signals.subtitle: EE ingests events from multiple data sources and computes deviation metrics for each.
signals.dependency_source: pip, npm, go, cargo, bundler lockfiles

adapters.title: Adapter Catalog
adapters.subtitle: Built-in adapters plus a growing plugin ecosystem. Each adapter reads from a data source and feeds it into the EE pipeline.
adapters.git_desc: Local git history analysis — commits, file changes, co-change patterns.
adapters.pip_desc: Python dependency tracking from requirements.txt and Pipfile.lock.
adapters.npm_desc: Node.js dependency tracking from package-lock.json with depth analysis.
adapters.go_desc: Go module tracking from go.sum with dependency depth.
adapters.cargo_desc: Rust crate tracking from Cargo.lock with depth analysis.
adapters.github_actions_desc: CI pipeline monitoring — run duration, failure rates, workflow trends.
adapters.github_releases_desc: Release cadence tracking, pre-release detection, asset monitoring.
adapters.dependabot_desc: Reads security advisory data from repos that use Dependabot alerts.
adapters.jenkins_desc: Reads CI build data from repos that use Jenkins.
adapters.gitlab_ci_desc: Reads CI/CD pipeline data from repos that use GitLab CI.
adapters.datadog_desc: Reads APM metrics, error rates, and deployment markers from repos that use Datadog.
adapters.sentry_desc: Reads error rates and release health data from repos that use Sentry.
adapters.sonarqube_desc: Reads quality gate status and code metrics from repos that use SonarQube.
adapters.snyk_desc: Reads vulnerability and license compliance data from repos that use Snyk.
adapters.pagerduty_desc: Reads incident frequency, MTTR, and escalation pattern data from teams that use PagerDuty.
adapters.jira_desc: Reads cycle time, WIP count, and sprint velocity from teams that use Jira.
adapters.argocd_desc: Reads GitOps deployment and sync status data from repos that use Argo CD.
adapters.build_your_own_desc: Create a custom adapter with our scaffold tool and step-by-step guide. Ship it as a pip package.
adapters.dont_see: Don't see your tool? We prioritize by community demand.

adapters_page.title: Adapter Ecosystem
adapters_page.subtitle: Built-in adapters, a plugin system for extensions, and an open ecosystem. Any tool with project data can become an adapter.
adapters_page.builtin_subtitle: Ships with Evolution Engine. Zero config, auto-detected from your repo.
adapters_page.openapi_desc: API schema change tracking from OpenAPI/Swagger specs.
adapters_page.docker_desc: Container config change tracking from Dockerfiles and compose files.
adapters_page.ci_local_desc: Local CI workflow analysis from GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, and CircleCI config files.
adapters_page.gitlab_pipelines_desc: GitLab CI/CD pipeline monitoring via API.
adapters_page.gitlab_releases_desc: GitLab release cadence tracking via API.
adapters_page.plugin_subtitle: Install any adapter via pip. Build your own in minutes. Plugins are auto-discovered at runtime.
adapters_page.install_desc: Third-party adapters are regular pip packages. Install one and EE discovers it automatically on the next run.
adapters_page.build_desc: Scaffold a new adapter in one command. Implement iter_events(), validate, and publish.
adapters_page.reference_desc: A working example adapter that reads test coverage data. Install it to see how plugins integrate with the pipeline.
adapters_page.ecosystem_subtitle: EE already detects these tools in your repo via prescan. Community adapters coming soon — or build one yourself.
adapters_page.open_ended: ...and any other tool with project data. The list above is non-exhaustive.
adapters_page.request_subtitle: We prioritize adapters by community demand. Tell us what you need, or build one yourself.

pricing.title: Simple, transparent pricing
pricing.subtitle: Start free. Upgrade when you need CI, deployment, and security signals.
pricing.free_desc: Forever free for git + dependency analysis
pricing.pro_desc: Full pipeline visibility with AI-powered investigation
pricing.billing_disclosure: Billed monthly. Cancel anytime. 30-day money-back guarantee.

consent.text: This site uses cookies for payment processing (Stripe) and optional anonymous telemetry. No tracking or advertising cookies are used.

footer.tagline: Your code never leaves your machine.

modal.title: Request an Adapter
modal.use_case_placeholder: What would this adapter help you detect or monitor?
modal.description_placeholder: API docs, data format, or anything else helpful...
modal.success_message: Request submitted! We'll track it as a GitHub issue.
modal.error_validation: Please fill in the adapter name and family.

datasources.title: Data Sources & Adapters
datasources.lead: The more sources connected, the richer the pattern detection. Every tool your team already uses can feed into Evolution Engine.
datasources.how_it_works_desc: Evolution Engine monitors your development process by analyzing signals from multiple sources and detecting cross-family patterns that no single tool can see.
datasources.integration_levels_desc: Evolution Engine delivers value at every level. Each additional data source unlocks new pattern families.
datasources.level1_what_you_need: What you need: A git repository. That’s it.
datasources.level1_item1: Commit-level signals: files touched, file dispersion, change locality, co-change novelty
datasources.level1_item2: Baseline comparison: “This commit touched 12 files — about 3x more than usual”
datasources.level1_item3: Risk advisories for unusually large, scattered, or novel changes
datasources.level2_what_you_need: What you need: A supported lockfile (package-lock.json, go.sum, Cargo.lock, Gemfile.lock, etc.).
datasources.level2_what_you_get: What you get: Everything in Level 1, plus:
datasources.level2_item2: Cross-family pattern: “Commits that change dependencies tend to touch 40% more spread-out files”
datasources.level3_what_you_need: What you need: GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, or CircleCI.
datasources.level3_what_you_get: What you get: Everything in Level 2, plus:
datasources.level3_item1: Build duration and failure rate tracking
datasources.level3_item2: Cross-family patterns: CI-triggering commits vs. code dispersion
datasources.level3_item3: Correlation between change size and build time trends
datasources.level4_what_you_need: What you need: GitHub Releases or GitLab Releases.
datasources.level4_what_you_get: What you get: Everything in Level 3, plus:
datasources.level4_item1: Release cadence, pre-release tracking, asset count monitoring
datasources.level4_item2: Cross-family patterns: release commits vs. file dispersion
datasources.level5_what_you_need: What you need: Any combination of the adapters below.
datasources.level5_sonarqube_example: “Low locality commits have more code smells”
datasources.value_equation_desc: Each new source doesn’t add linearly — it multiplies the pattern space.
datasources.tokens_desc: Token-based adapters (CI, deployments, security) need an API token to fetch data. Set these as environment variables before running evo analyze.
datasources.troubleshooting_title: Troubleshooting: Token Set but No Data?
datasources.troubleshooting_desc: If your report shows "Token set but no data collected" or "Connected — No Data", here’s what to check for each adapter.
datasources.troubleshooting_ci_title: CI / Build (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI)
datasources.troubleshooting_ci_item1: Need pipeline runs. EE reads the last 90 days of CI runs. If the repo has no workflow runs in that window, there’s nothing to analyze.
datasources.troubleshooting_ci_item2: Check: Go to your repo’s Actions tab (GitHub) or CI/CD → Pipelines (GitLab) and confirm runs exist.
datasources.troubleshooting_ci_item4: Requires Pro for API-based CI adapters. See pricing.
datasources.troubleshooting_deploy_item1: Need published releases. Git tags alone are not enough — you need actual Releases with release notes.
datasources.troubleshooting_deploy_item2: Check: Go to your repo’s Releases page and confirm published releases exist.
datasources.troubleshooting_security_item1: Need Dependabot enabled. Go to repo Settings → Code security and analysis → enable Dependabot alerts.
datasources.troubleshooting_security_item2: No alerts = no data. If Dependabot is enabled but finds no vulnerabilities, there’s nothing to report (which is good!).
datasources.troubleshooting_sentry_item1: Need events in the project. EE queries the last 90 days of Sentry issues.
datasources.troubleshooting_sentry_item2: Check env vars: All three are required: SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN, SENTRY_ORG, SENTRY_PROJECT.
datasources.troubleshooting_testing_item1: File-based — no token needed. These adapters look for report files in your repo.
datasources.troubleshooting_testing_item3: CI artifacts: If your test/coverage reports are generated during CI but not committed, they won’t be found locally. Either commit them or run evo analyze in CI where the files exist.
datasources.troubleshooting_platform_desc: If you see "Platform Mismatch", it means your token doesn’t match the git remote. For example, GITLAB_TOKEN is set but the repo’s remote points to github.com. Set the correct token for your hosting platform, or unset the mismatched one.
datasources.callout_diagnostic: Quick diagnostic: Run evo sources to see exactly which adapters are connected, which need tokens, and which need a Pro license.
datasources.what_if_title: Check What More Sources Would Add
datasources.what_if_desc: Entirely optional. Evolution Engine works standalone with just git. But if you’re curious:
datasources.callout_what_if: You can compare reports side by side — run once without adapters, once with — and see if the additional patterns are worth it for your team.
datasources.faq_q1: Do I need to replace my existing tools?
datasources.faq_a1: No. Evolution Engine sits alongside your tools, not instead of them. It ingests their output as signals and finds patterns across all of them.
datasources.faq_q2: What if I only have git?
datasources.faq_a2: That’s enough to start. Git-only analysis detects commit-level anomalies. Many teams start here and add sources over time.
datasources.faq_q3: Does my code leave my machine?
datasources.faq_a3: No. Evolution Engine runs locally. Code, events, and signals stay on your machine. The only optional network calls are for API-based adapters and the opt-in community knowledge base.
datasources.faq_q4: How is this different from Snyk / SonarQube / Datadog?
datasources.faq_a4: Those tools analyze individual events at a point in time. Evolution Engine analyzes the process over time — detecting gradual drift, cross-family correlations, and patterns that only emerge across hundreds of commits. Their output can feed into EE as additional signal families.
datasources.diagram:
Your existing tools          Evolution Engine              What you get
─────────────────           ─────────────────             ──────────────
Git history          ──➤    ┌─────────────────┐
CI/CD (Actions)      ──➤    │  Signal Engine   │──➤  Cross-family patterns
Dependencies (npm)   ──➤    │  (Phase 2)       │──➤  Risk advisories
Security (Snyk)      ──➤    │                  │──➤  Drift detection
Deployments          ──➤    │  Pattern Engine  │──➤  Trend reports
Monitoring           ──➤    │  (Phase 4)       │──➤  Investigation prompts
Quality gates        ──➤    └─────────────────┘

docs_page.title: Documentation
docs_page.lead: Everything you need to get started with Evolution Engine.
docs_page.quickstart_analyze_desc: EE auto-detects your tools (git history, lockfiles, CI configs) and runs the full 5-phase pipeline: events, signals, explanations, patterns, and advisory.
docs_page.quickstart_sources: See what data sources are available
docs_page.quickstart_sources_desc: Shows connected adapters and tools detected in your repo that could provide additional signals.
docs_page.integration_desc: EE supports three integration paths. Start with the CLI, graduate to hooks when you trust the output, and add the GitHub Action for team-wide coverage.
docs_page.path1_desc: Already covered above. Run evo analyze . whenever you want to check on your repo.
docs_page.path2_desc: Git hooks run analysis on every commit or push automatically. The hook runs in the background and never blocks your commit.
docs_page.path2_hook_intro: When the hook detects significant changes, it:
docs_page.path2_hook_notify: Sends a desktop notification (macOS and Linux)
docs_page.path2_hook_report: Opens the HTML report in your browser
docs_page.path2_hook_snapshot: Saves a history snapshot for later comparison
docs_page.path2_config_desc: Customize hook behavior with evo config set:
docs_page.path2_watcher_desc: For repos where git hooks aren't practical, use the commit watcher:
docs_page.path3_desc: Add Evolution Engine to your pull request workflow. Every PR gets an analysis comment with risk badges.
docs_page.path3_action_comment: Posts a summary comment with risk levels and evidence
docs_page.path3_action_investigate: Optionally runs AI investigation on High/Critical findings
docs_page.path3_action_compare: Compares against previous analysis on subsequent pushes
docs_page.path3_action_inline: Posts inline fix suggestions as review comments
docs_page.callout_paths: All three paths work together. Use hooks for instant local feedback, the Action for team-wide PR coverage, and the CLI for ad-hoc deep dives. Run evo init . --path all to set up everything at once.
docs_page.cmd_analyze: evo analyze [path] — Run the full pipeline (detect adapters, compute signals, generate advisory)
docs_page.cmd_sources: evo sources [path] — Show connected and detected data sources
docs_page.cmd_sources_whatif: evo sources --what-if datadog — Estimate impact of adding an adapter
docs_page.cmd_status: evo status [path] — Show detected adapters and last run info
docs_page.cmd_investigate: evo investigate [path] — AI investigation of advisory findings
docs_page.cmd_investigate_prompt: evo investigate . --show-prompt — Print the prompt for manual use
docs_page.cmd_fix: evo fix [path] — AI fix loop (iterate until advisory clears)
docs_page.cmd_fix_dry: evo fix . --dry-run — Preview fix prompt without modifying files
docs_page.cmd_adapter_list: evo adapter list — List detected adapters with trust badges
docs_page.cmd_adapter_discover: evo adapter discover [path] — Find available adapters for tools in your repo
docs_page.cmd_adapter_new: evo adapter new <name> --family ci — Scaffold a new adapter package
docs_page.cmd_adapter_validate: evo adapter validate <path> — Validate an adapter against the contract
docs_page.cmd_adapter_guide: evo adapter guide — Show the full adapter building guide
docs_page.cmd_adapter_prompt: evo adapter prompt <name> --family ci — Generate an AI prompt to build an adapter
docs_page.cmd_adapter_block: evo adapter block <name> -r "reason" — Block an adapter locally
docs_page.cmd_adapter_unblock: evo adapter unblock <name> — Unblock a blocked adapter
docs_page.cmd_adapter_updates: evo adapter check-updates — Check PyPI for plugin updates
docs_page.cmd_adapter_report: evo adapter report <name> — Report a broken/malicious adapter
docs_page.cmd_notif_list: evo notifications list — Show pending update notifications
docs_page.cmd_notif_check: evo notifications check [path] — Force a notification check (ignores 24h cache)
docs_page.cmd_patterns_list: evo patterns list — Show patterns in the knowledge base
docs_page.cmd_patterns_packages: evo patterns packages — List pattern packages and cache status
docs_page.cmd_patterns_new: evo patterns new <name> — Scaffold a new pattern package
docs_page.cmd_patterns_validate: evo patterns validate <path> — Validate a pattern package
docs_page.cmd_patterns_publish: evo patterns publish <path> — Publish pattern package to PyPI
docs_page.cmd_patterns_add: evo patterns add <package> — Subscribe to a pattern package
docs_page.cmd_patterns_remove: evo patterns remove <package> — Unsubscribe from a pattern package
docs_page.cmd_patterns_block: evo patterns block <name> — Block a pattern package
docs_page.cmd_patterns_unblock: evo patterns unblock <name> — Unblock a pattern package
docs_page.cmd_patterns_pull: evo patterns pull [path] — Fetch community patterns from the registry
docs_page.cmd_patterns_push: evo patterns push [path] — Share anonymized patterns (requires privacy_level ≥ 1)
docs_page.cmd_patterns_export: evo patterns export — Export anonymized pattern digests
docs_page.cmd_history_list: evo history list [path] — Show past run snapshots
docs_page.cmd_history_show: evo history show <run> — View a specific run's advisory
docs_page.cmd_history_diff: evo history diff [r1 r2] — Compare two runs
docs_page.cmd_history_clean: evo history clean --keep N — Delete old snapshots
docs_page.safety_desc: Every adapter is assigned a trust badge automatically:
docs_page.safety_builtin: [built-in] — Ships with Evolution Engine, maintained by the core team
docs_page.safety_verified: [verified] — Published on PyPI and reviewed by EE maintainers
docs_page.safety_community: [community] — Published on PyPI, not yet reviewed
docs_page.safety_block_desc: Adapters can be blocked locally (evo adapter block) or globally via the bundled blocklist. Use evo adapter report to flag broken or malicious adapters.
docs_page.cmd_hooks_install: evo hooks install [path] — Install git hooks for auto-analysis
docs_page.cmd_hooks_status: evo hooks status [path] — Show hook installation status and config
docs_page.cmd_setup_ui: evo setup --ui — Open settings page in browser
docs_page.cmd_config_set: evo config set <key> <value> — Set a config value
docs_page.cmd_license_status: evo license status — Show current license tier
docs_page.cmd_license_activate: evo license activate <key> — Activate a Pro license
docs_page.cmd_telemetry: evo telemetry on|off|status — Manage anonymous usage telemetry
docs_page.discovery_desc: EE includes a lightweight notification system that keeps you informed about available adapters, updates, and community patterns. All checks are privacy-respecting — they only read public PyPI metadata and never send data from your machine.
docs_page.discovery_adapter_desc: EE scans your repo for known tools (via config files, lockfiles, and import statements) and checks PyPI for matching adapter packages:
docs_page.discovery_notif_desc: After evo analyze completes, EE checks for adapter updates and new adapters matching detected tools. Notifications appear at the end of the output:
docs_page.discovery_patterns_desc: Patterns accumulate organically as users analyze repos and share discoveries. EE distributes community patterns through two redundant channels:
docs_page.discovery_patterns_registry: Registry (real-time) — patterns pushed by users are immediately available to all other users on their next evo analyze
docs_page.discovery_patterns_pypi: PyPI packages (durable) — periodic snapshots published as pip packages, auto-fetched without pip install
docs_page.discovery_patterns_fallback: If the registry is unavailable, PyPI packages still work. Both channels are checked automatically.
docs_page.discovery_default_pkg_desc: The canonical community pattern package is evo-patterns-community, published using CalVer (e.g. 2026.2.15). It contains aggregated patterns from the community registry and is auto-fetched on every evo analyze.
docs_page.discovery_autofetch_desc: Pattern packages are pip packages on PyPI containing pure data (no executable code). EE downloads the wheel directly, extracts patterns.json, validates it, and imports relevant patterns automatically — no pip install required.
docs_page.discovery_autofetch_cache: Pattern packages are cached at ~/.evo/pattern_cache.json with a 24-hour TTL. Only patterns matching your locally-detected families are imported.
docs_page.discovery_push_pull_desc: Share your anonymized patterns with the community and pull patterns from others:
docs_page.discovery_push_pull_validation: The registry (codequal.dev/api/patterns) validates all submissions server-side. Patterns require attestations from 2+ independent instances before they are fully trusted.
docs_page.discovery_blocklist_desc: Block unwanted pattern packages from being imported:
docs_page.discovery_blocklist_storage: Blocked packages are stored at ~/.evo/pattern_blocklist.json. A bundled blocklist ships with EE for known-bad packages.
docs_page.discovery_config_desc: All update and notification behavior is configurable:
docs_page.discovery_config_dnt: Set DO_NOT_TRACK=1 in your environment to disable all network checks.
docs_page.callout_pypi: How PyPI discovery works: EE queries the public PyPI JSON API (https://pypi.org/pypi/{package}/json) using Python's stdlib urllib.request. No API keys or authentication needed. Results are cached for 24 hours at ~/.evo/version_cache.json.
docs_page.build_adapter_desc: Adapters read from external data sources and feed events into the Evolution Engine pipeline. Each adapter produces events that EE processes through its 5-phase analysis. You can build an adapter for any tool — CI systems, monitoring platforms, issue trackers, deployment tools, etc.
docs_page.callout_review: Adapters you build and submit are reviewed by the EE team and, if approved, bundled for all users in future releases.
docs_page.build_step1_desc: Use the built-in scaffold tool to create a ready-to-publish pip package:
docs_page.build_step1_creates: This creates a package directory with:
docs_page.build_step1_adapter: evo_jenkins/adapter.py — Your adapter class (fill in the logic)
docs_page.build_step1_toml: pyproject.toml — Package metadata with the EE plugin entry point
docs_page.build_step2_desc: Your adapter must implement the Adapter Contract. The key method is fetch_events(), which yields event dicts:
docs_page.build_step4_desc: Once published, anyone can install it with pip install evo-adapter-jenkins and EE will auto-detect it via the plugin entry point.
docs_page.build_ai_desc: Generate a complete prompt for your AI coding assistant:
docs_page.build_ai_detail: This produces a detailed prompt with the full adapter contract, examples, and a validation checklist. Paste it into Claude, ChatGPT, Cursor, or any AI tool.
docs_page.contract_desc: Every adapter must satisfy these requirements to pass evo adapter validate:
docs_page.contract_method_init: __init__(self, repo_path, **kwargs) — Constructor. Must accept repo_path as first argument.
docs_page.contract_method_detect: detect(self) -> bool — Returns True if this adapter's data source is available in the repo.
docs_page.contract_method_fetch: fetch_events(self, since=None, limit=100) -> Iterator[dict] — Yields event dicts.
docs_page.contract_entry_desc: In your pyproject.toml, register the entry point so EE can auto-discover your adapter:
docs_page.submit_desc: Once your adapter passes validation and is published on PyPI:
docs_page.submit_step1: Test it on at least one real repository
docs_page.submit_step2: Ensure evo adapter validate passes with no errors
docs_page.submit_step3_desc: A brief description of what it does
docs_page.submit_step4: We review the adapter, run our test suite against it, and if approved, bundle it in a future EE release for all users
docs_page.callout_submit: Accepted adapters get a "Community" badge in the adapter catalog and credit in the release notes.
docs_page.contract_entrypoint_desc: In your pyproject.toml, register the entry point so EE can auto-discover your adapter:
docs_page.callout_accepted: Accepted adapters get a "Community" badge in the adapter catalog and credit in the release notes.
